question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what additional support his Department will provide Cricket Ireland in advance of the first five-day Test Match to be played in Belfast in July 2024.

To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her discussions with the PSNI and the Policing Board regarding improving the process for police officers applying for Injury on Duty and Ill Health Retirement.

To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to provide funding for households to receive support for the installation of heat pumps, similar to what is available to households in England and Wales.
answer_text: My department is currently preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps. https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/economy/Energy-Strategy-2023-Action-Plan-Report.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what measures will be taken to commission and commence a review of animal welfare services.
answer_text: Enforcement of animal welfare legislation is carried out by the PSNI for wild animals, councils for non-farmed animal and my department for farmed animals. The PSNI and councils decide how best to deliver their statutory obligations and my Department has no recourse to intervene. To do so would undermine the legal and operational authority of the PSNI and councils.  If the member has specific concerns relating to animal welfare services I would encourage him to raise these matters with my Department, PSNI or District Councils as relevant.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what health and safety measures are in place for staff working in the Search Facility with an overhead metal detector and x-ray machine.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is very aware of its responsibilities under the Health and Safety at Work Act, and as such has carried out a radiation risk assessment for all x-ray screening equipment.In addition to this, the Assembly Commission has registered the equipment with HSENI under the Ionising Radiation Regulations (NI) 2017.  All users have received training and a Radiation Advisor has been appointed for the building.  Trained radiation supervisors carry out regular checks of the equipment and these are retained for record.The equipment is maintained and tested regularly as required, and a yearly radiation inspection takes place to ensure that they continue to operate within safe working limits.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of progress in delivering an outcomes-based Programme for Government.
answer_text: Work to develop a Programme for Government is moving forward quickly. The Executive is agreed on priorities, including: childcare; reducing hospital waiting lists; tackling violence against women and girls; Special Educational Needs; housing; developing a globally competitive economy; and Reform and Transformation of public services. We will move at pace to agree a programme and bring it before the Assembly for scrutiny and approval. We will keep Members updated as progress continues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will commission a review of animal breeding, to ensure those who are breeding have licences and are declaring income from animal sales, specifically dogs.
answer_text: Any dog breeding establishment, which is operating on a commercial basis, will require a licence from the relevant council in which they are located. A person who keeps only one or two breeding bitches is classified as a hobby breeder and does not have to obtain a dog breeding licence. I am acutely aware of the need for animal welfare reform across a range of areas. I am committed to reviewing animal welfare legislation and will be engaging with my officials to establish priorities and to determine what legislation can be delivered within resource constraints and the lifetime of the current mandate.While I have no current plans for a formal review of legal and illegal dog breeding, I am conscious that more action may be required in the time ahead and so I am keen to work with stakeholders on this and other related issues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what actions his Department will take to implement an animal welfare strategy.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the need for animal welfare reforms and will be considering options with stakeholders and my officials in the time ahead. This will assist me to determine what is needed, and what is deliverable, within the remainder of this mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what plans there are to facilitate more car parking at Parliament Buildings for staff and visitors.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission has the use of three car parks, two of which are adjacent to Parliament Buildings, with a third and much larger car park, known as the lower east or visitor car park, situated in relatively close proximity to the Search Facility.The Assembly Commission acknowledges the difficulties experienced with parking in these car parks particularly on sitting days, and Assembly Commission management are continuing to look at ways in which these difficulties might be eased.Vehicular access to the two upper car parks adjacent to the Parliament Buildings is managed on a daily basis by staff from Usher Services, who ensure that only those persons authorised to access these car parks do so.Assembly Commission management are also looking at ways in which parking within the lower east or visitor car park might be improved, and Usher Services will be monitoring the car park on a regular basis from 8 April 2024. It is hoped that this may lead to improved parking and the easier movement of vehicles therein. An overspill car park in close proximity to the lower east or visitor car park should also be available from that date to assist with pressures faced on busier days.In conclusion, the Assembly Commission will continue to keep this matter under close review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what animal welfare inspections have been carried out at the puppy farm in Fivemiletown to ensure basic needs are being met, as outlined by the Five Freedoms of Animal Welfare.
answer_text: Under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, responsibility for enforcing the law, as it relates to non-farmed animals, rests with local councils. My department has therefore passed your request to Fermanagh and Omagh District Council and asked that they respond to you directly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the staff ratios in place for animal boarding kennels.
answer_text: Licensing of animal boarding establishment is regulated by the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 1972 and the Animal Boarding Establishment Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1974. Neither piece of legislation contains specific requirements for staffing ratios. 		In January 2022, the Department published a report commissioned from the Canine Socialisation Expert Working Group (CSEWG) which stated that staff to dog ratios in licensed kennels should not exceed 10 dogs per staff member. My department continues to use this guidance when inspecting and licensing dog boarding establishments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what actions his Department will take to investigate and inspect licenced breeding establishments to ensure the Five Freedoms of Animal Welfare are being met.
answer_text: Responsibility for inspecting and licensing dog breeding establishments rests solely with local councils under The Welfare of Animals (Dog Breeding Establishments and Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations 2013.  These regulations are derived from The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, which enshrines the five freedoms of animal welfare in law.  Any licenced breeding establishment must ensure it is compliant with all prevailing animal welfare legislation, including the 2011 Act.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action his Department will take to address animal welfare issues in instances where businesses are exploiting animals, especially dogs, for economic gain.
answer_text: Any person operating a pet shop, a livery yard, or a zoo and any person who provides boarding for animals or commercially breeds and sells dogs must be inspected and must have a licence, prior to commencing business.  Furthermore, all animals under the care of control of these businesses are protected by the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.  Individuals breaching licensing laws or welfare rules are liable to investigation and enforcement action by the relevant statutory authority.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement his Department has had with Fermanagh and Omagh District Council in relation to the puppy farm in Fivemiletown continuing as a legal, licenced and registered business, given the animal welfare concerns that have been raised.
answer_text: My department has no role in the inspection or licensing of any dog breeding establishment nor is there a requirement for any council to consult with my Department when deciding to grant a licence. If a person has evidence that any dog breeding establishment is operating in breach of its licensing conditions, I urge that person to raise those concerns directly with the council responsible for granting that licence.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his officials are taking to ensure a fuller picture is collated of air pollution hotspots.
answer_text: My department financially supports district councils to review and assess a range of air pollutants against the objectives set by legislation, using a range of monitoring, modelling, observations and corresponding analyses. For locations where objectives are not expected to be met by the relevant target date, district councils are required to declare an Air Quality Management Area, and to develop an action plan addressing the problem. An annual report is also published by DAERA, which includes a summary of compliance for the pollutants measured at each air quality monitoring site in Northern Ireland against legislative targets, strategy objectives and WHO Air Quality Guidelines.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any cross-departmental discussions aimed at reducing air pollution and improving lung health.
answer_text: My Department takes a pro-active approach to cross-departmental engagement to reduce air pollution. My officials routinely liaise with colleagues from Department for Economy, Department for Infrastructure, and Department of Health.An Inter-Departmental Steering Group has also been established to discuss the proposed actions in Northern Ireland’s forthcoming Clean Air Strategy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on progress towards delivering on the draft Clean Air Strategy.
answer_text: Officials have been progressing this work in the absence of an Executive. Now that the Executive has returned, I will work with my officials and Ministerial colleagues to finalise this important cross-cutting Strategy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions that have taken place to prioritise the issue of clean air in the Programme for Government.
answer_text: It is clear that a new Programme for Government and indeed a new Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland will need to reflect and support delivery of all of the requirements of the Climate Change (NI) Act 2022 including those relating to air quality.  My officials continue to work with their colleagues in the Executive Office in developing advice and proposals to inform the Executive’s decisions on the approach to be taken to the Programme for Government.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of his Department's strategy A 10X Economy.

To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for the cost of the granite paving road surface; (ii) how long the surface lasted; and (iii) the budget for the new, tarmac road surface, on Queens Square, Belfast.
answer_text: The Queen’s Square/Custom House Square area was originally created by the Laganside Corporation, with construction completing in 2006. The Laganside Corporation dissolved in 2007, with its assets and responsibilities transferring to the former Department for Social Development. The Department for Communities does not hold the cost of the granite paving road surface as this information was not transferred to the Department at the time of the dissolution. Construction of the Queen’s Square/Custom House Square area was completed in 2006.The budget for the new tarmac road surface is £206,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how her Department ensures, measures and evaluates how the Planning Appeals Commission prioritises its resources, in line with the priorities and policy objectives of the Northern Ireland Executive.
answer_text: While the Department of Justice is the sponsor body, the Planning Appeals Commission is a wholly independent appellant body in terms of the operation of appeals, hearings, and inquiries in compliance with the Planning Act (NI) 2011.The Commission determines its priorities, and allocation of resources in accordance with its statutory responsibilities. Therefore, it would not be appropriate for me as Minister of Justice to comment on matters that encroach on that independence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what measurements or review mechanisms does her Department use to manage and assess the performance of the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: While the Department of Justice is the sponsor Department, the Planning Appeals Commission is a wholly independent appellant body in terms of the operation of appeals, hearings, and inquiries in compliance with the Planning Act (NI) 2011. The 2021/22 Annual Report includes the Commission’s assessment of performance. It would not be appropriate for me as Minister of Justice to comment on matters that encroach on the independence of the Chief Commissioner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the performance of the Planning Appeals Commission, in light of its Annual Review 2021/22 which details that decision-making times for planning appeals have almost doubled since 2019/20, whilst its budget increased by almost 30 percent over the same time period.
answer_text: While the Department of Justice is the sponsor body, the Planning Appeals Commission is a wholly independent appellant body in terms of the operation of appeals, hearings, and inquiries in compliance with the Planning Act (NI) 2011. The Department of Justice sets the financial governance framework, however it is for the Commission to determine priorities, processes, activities, and expenditure in accordance with its statutory responsibilities. Therefore, it would not be appropriate for me as Minister of Justice to comment on matters that encroach on the independence of the Chief Commissioner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail how the Planning Appeals Commission is held to account; and (ii) for her assessment of the appropriateness of the oversight arrangements, compared to other independent bodies under her Department’s remit.
answer_text: (i) the Planning Appeals Commission has not been included in any recent reviews of the planning system.(ii) Reform to planning legislation falls to the Department for Infrastructure (DfI). My officials advise that DfI officials and the Chief Commissioner maintain regular contact so that the Commission can consider the impact on its functions of any new legislation, changes in regional policy or guidance related to the planning system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether the Planning Appeals Commission has been included in any reviews of Northern Ireland’s planning system; and (ii) to detail how the Planning Appeals Commission is being included in plans to reform the planning system.
answer_text: (i) the Planning Appeals Commission has not been included in any recent reviews of the planning system.(ii) Reform to planning legislation falls to the Department for Infrastructure (DfI). My officials advise that DfI officials and the Chief Commissioner maintain regular contact so that the Commission can consider the impact on its functions of any new legislation, changes in regional policy or guidance related to the planning system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact of the Voluntary Exit Scheme on her Department.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) launched a voluntary exit scheme on 2 March 2015.  It reduced the size of the pay bill by around £24m in the 2015/16 financial year and ongoing annual savings in the region of £87m thereafter.  This was achieved through a reduction of around 2,360 posts (in full time equivalent terms) across the NICS which equated to around 3,000 people.  The scheme was designed to maximise pay bill savings while minimising costs and protecting business continuity; it played a significant part in enabling each Department to operate within it its budget allocation. There was no individual assessment on the impact on my Department.  However, while the impact of the loss of almost 3,000 staff across the NICS in a relatively short period should not be under estimated, the design of the scheme sought to minimise disruption to service delivery by the phased approach to exits. In addition, the selection of applicants for release was on a departmental basis with a view to fulfilling each department’s requirements in terms of the size of reductions required and the grade/discipline composition of that reduction required to achieve the necessary departmental savings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the impact of the Voluntary Exit Scheme on his Department.
answer_text: The NICS launched a voluntary exit scheme on 2 March 2015. This reduced the size of the pay bill by around £24m in the 2015/16 financial year and ongoing annual savings in the region of £87m thereafter. This was achieved through a reduction of around 2,360 posts (in full time equivalent terms) which equated to around 3,000 people. The scheme was designed to maximise pay bill savings while minimising costs and protecting business continuity; it played a significant part in enabling each Department to operate within it its budget allocation. There was no individual assessment on the impact to my Department.  However across NICS while the impact of the loss of almost 3,000 staff in a relatively short period should not be under estimated, the design of the scheme sought to minimise disruption to service delivery by the phased approach to exits. In addition, the selection of applicants for release was on a departmental basis with a view to fulfilling each department’s requirements in terms of the size of reductions required and the grade/discipline composition of that reduction required to achieve the necessary departmental savings.

answer_text: The NICS launched a voluntary exit scheme on 2 March 2015. This reduced the size of the pay bill by around £24m in the 2015/16 financial year and ongoing annual savings in the region of £87m thereafter. This was achieved through a reduction of around 2,360 posts (in full time equivalent terms) which equated to around 3,000 people. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact of the Voluntary Exit Scheme on his Department.
answer_text: The NICS launched a voluntary exit scheme on 2 March 2015. This reduced the size of the pay bill by around £24m in the 2015/16 financial year and ongoing annual savings in the region of £87m thereafter. This was achieved through a reduction of around 2,360 posts (in full time equivalent terms) which equated to around 3,000 people. The scheme was designed to maximise pay bill savings while minimising costs and protecting business continuity; it played a significant part in enabling each Department to operate within it its budget allocation.The Department for the Economy was established subsequent to the voluntary exit scheme and no assessment was undertaken of the impact of the scheme on the constituent parts that came together to form the new Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of the impact of the Voluntary Exit Scheme on her Department.
answer_text: As a result of the 2015 NICS Voluntary Exit Scheme, 78.6 Full Time Equivalent Staff in the Department of Education left the Northern Ireland Civil Service.  This represented 13.4% of DE staff in post at October 2014.The NICS People Survey Employee Engagement Index for DE has shown an improvement in staff satisfaction year-on-year since 2017, which indicates that reductions through VES have had no negative impact on staff morale.The Department engaged with the Northern Ireland Audit Office in 2016 having assessed the impact of VES on service delivery. The narrative of the impact related primarily to budgetary requirements to reduce costs in relatively short timeframe and the associated risks of undertaking this.  Since 2016, there have been no additional formal studies conducted at Departmental level to assess the impact of VES on service delivery in DE.  However, Senior Officials within DE regularly review the staffing levels within the Department to ensure ongoing business needs are met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the outages in the provision of TranslinkWiFi on the Glider service since it’s inception, broken down by (i) number of outages; (ii) average length of outages; and (iii) outages as a percentage of overall provision of TranslinkWiFi.
answer_text: Details regarding the provision of Wi-Fi on the Glider service or outages in respect of same would be an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the known issues with the sewer network in the Hazlewood Lane and Oakwood Park area of Lisbane, Comber; (ii) what solutions he has identified for fixing the problems; (iii) the associated costs for the upgrades required; and (iv) the timeline to complete necessary work.
answer_text: Issues with the sewer network across the North and any associated remediation work is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why only 21 of Northern Ireland's 39 special schools receive funding for summer schemes.
answer_text: The Department, as part of its ongoing engagement with Special School Strategic Leadership Group (SSSLG), has recognised this gap in summer provisions across special schools and has made additional funding available to support the development of summer provisions within the other 18 special schools during the   Summer 2021 DE Summer Scheme Funding, with a further £700K for Summer 2022.The Education Authority (EA) provides historic funding to special schools for summer schemes, specifically for those pupils who have severe and profound learning difficulties. Currently there are 21 of the 39 special schools offering summer schemes, and in receipt of this funding. Those schools were previously recognised as designated Severe Learning Disabilities (SLD) schools. In recent years, due to increasingly more complex pupil profiles, the number of special schools catering for severe and profound learning needs has risen beyond the 21 formally designated SLD schools. I have asked my officials to work with the EA, SSSLG, the wider special school sector and other key stakeholders during the forthcoming Academic Year 2022/23 to review the summer scheme provision, to shape a more equitable and regional approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for the total spend to date on the Energy Strategy; and (ii) what the strategy has funded.
answer_text: The development of the Energy Strategy started in 2019 with the Call for Evidence. There followed wide-ranging engagement across government and with external stakeholders leading to a formal consultation on options in 2021. Therefore, the production of the Energy Strategy included a significant amount of work by officials across the range of departments in government. The cost of this resource has not been quantified. During the current financial year (22/23) planned external public sector investment in the delivery of the Energy Strategy includes:£15m in ‘invest to save’ projects in central government premises, which will lower the energy costs to taxpayers; £10m for Green Innovation Challenge Fund to support research and innovation in zero carbon technologies; andThe intention to launch a multi-year energy-efficiency intervention flagship as part of the energy decarbonisation pathway but also to tackle social need and generate skills development and local employment opportunities.
